planting.note: Direct sow once soil can be worked; sow again in mid to late summer for a fall crop.

directions: Danvers 126 is a dependable heirloom that produces tapered, deep-orange roots even in heavier or imperfect soils. Loosen the bed deeply and remove stones so roots stay uniform, then sow seed thinly about a quarter inch deep. Keep the surface consistently moist during the slow germination period, and thin seedlings to a couple inches apart once they establish. Harvest at roughly 70 to 80 days when shoulders reach three-quarters of an inch, and hold roots in cool storage for winter use.

planting.note: Sow after all danger of frost has passed and soil has warmed.

directions: This 1894 heirloom develops oblong fruits with hard cream skin striped in green and sweet, tender flesh. Sow seed an inch deep in warm soil, spacing plants generously to give the semi-vining habit room to run. Provide steady moisture through fruit set and mulch to conserve water and suppress weeds. Harvest when the skin hardens and the stripes deepen, then cure fruits in a warm spot for a week or two to improve storage and flavor.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer for a fall harvest.

directions: Detroit Dark Red is a classic heirloom prized for deep crimson roots, tender texture, and sweet flavor that holds up for canning and storage. Sow seed about half an inch deep in loose, well-drained soil, keeping the bed evenly moist to aid the multi-seeded clusters. Thin seedlings to three or four inches apart so roots size up well, and use the thinnings as tender greens. Pull roots at two to three inches for the best eating quality, and hold larger roots in cool, damp storage over winter.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer for fall harvest.

directions: This golden heirloom beet offers vibrant orange roots with a mild, buttery, non-bleeding flavor and edible greens. Sow seed half an inch deep in loose, stone-free soil and keep the surface moist until the seedlings emerge. Thin to three or four inches apart so roots develop fully, and harvest greens sparingly along the way. Roast, steam, or eat roots raw, and store surplus in a cool, humid spot for winter keeping.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer for fall harvest.

directions: A beloved heirloom with sweet, non-bleeding orange roots and nutritious edible greens that stay mild whether raw or cooked. Direct sow seed clusters half an inch deep in fertile, well-drained soil, keeping moisture steady during germination. Thin seedlings to a few inches apart and remove weeds so roots can swell evenly. Harvest at golf-ball to tennis-ball size for tender eating, and cure larger roots for cool storage.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant, or time as a fall crop to mature in cool weather.

directions: This rare Italian heirloom forms striking violet-purple curds with a tender texture and mild, subtly nutty flavor. Start seed indoors about six weeks before setting out, then transplant into rich, evenly moist soil for steady, uninterrupted growth. Feed regularly and keep water consistent, since stress can cause loose or bitter heads. Cut heads while the curds are firm and richly colored, knowing the purple may soften toward green when cooked.

planting.note: Sow after frost in warm soil; a trellis keeps the long-handled fruits straight.

directions: This heirloom hardshell gourd produces distinctive long-handled fruits reaching up to two feet, useful for ladles, birdhouses, and crafts. Sow seed an inch deep in warm soil and give the vigorous vines a sturdy trellis so the handles hang straight. Water steadily through the long season and let fruits fully mature on the vine. Harvest after the stems dry and cure the gourds in a dry, airy space for several weeks until the shells harden and rattle.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring or late summer; flavor sweetens after frost.

directions: This compact heirloom kale grows low and wide with finely curled green leaves that stay sweet and mild, and it tolerates both cold and heat. Sow seed a quarter to half an inch deep in fertile soil, thinning to give plants room to spread. Harvest outer leaves for baby greens or let plants size up for cut-and-come-again picking. Cold weather and light frost improve the flavor, making it ideal for containers, raised beds, and small-space fall gardens.

planting.note: Direct sow as soon as soil can be worked in early spring.

directions: This early Dutch heirloom shelling pea charms with pink blooms and vivid purple pods on compact, productive vines. Sow seed an inch deep as soon as the soil is workable, since peas thrive in cool spring weather. Provide a short trellis or brushy support to keep the vines tidy and the pods clean. Harvest the purple pods while plump and shell out the sweet green peas promptly, as flavor is best right after picking.

planting.note: Sow in summer and overwinter plants for late winter to spring sprouts.

directions: Unlike single-head types, this cold-hardy heirloom produces a flush of deep purple florets on many side shoots for an extended harvest. Start seed in summer and set robust transplants into rich soil so they establish before winter. Plants overwinter in mild climates and reward growers with tender sprouts in late winter and early spring. Pick shoots regularly while the buds are tight to keep new sprouts coming; a brief cook preserves the color.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant for spring, or sow midsummer for fall heads.

directions: This 19th-century heirloom produces compact, round green heads with a mild flavor and dependable cold tolerance. Start seed indoors about five to six weeks before transplanting into firm, fertile soil. Keep watering even and steady, since fluctuations can split maturing heads. Harvest once heads feel solid and firm, cutting cleanly at the base; it performs well in both spring and fall plantings.

planting.note: Sow after frost when soil is warm; soaking seed overnight speeds germination.

directions: This heirloom okra is prized for smooth, rich green pods that stay tender even when they grow larger than usual, over a long harvest window. Sow seed about an inch deep in warm soil, soaking overnight first to hasten sprouting. Give plants full sun and steady moisture, and they will grow tall and productive through the heat of summer. Pick pods every day or two while young to keep the plants setting; frequent harvest greatly increases total yield.

planting.note: Sow after frost in warm soil; allow ample room for sprawling vines.

directions: Also called Musquee de Provence, this heirloom produces deeply lobed pumpkins that ripen from green to warm chestnut-brown with rich, flavorful flesh favored by cooks. Sow seed an inch deep in warm, fertile soil and give the long vines plenty of room to sprawl. Water consistently and side-dress with compost as fruits swell. Let pumpkins ripen fully to their bronze color, then cure in a warm spot to deepen flavor and extend storage.

planting.note: Direct sow in warm soil; succession sow for a steady supply of greens.

directions: This dual-purpose heirloom herb, also called methi, is grown for both its aromatic leafy greens and its spice seeds. Direct sow seed a quarter inch deep in warm, well-drained soil, since this legume resents transplanting. Harvest young leaves within about a month for cooking, or leave plants to flower and set the small pods. For seed, let pods dry on the plant, then collect and store the golden seeds for culinary and traditional herbal use.

planting.note: Best sown for a fall crop to avoid bolting in summer heat.

directions: Also known as finocchio, this cool-season crop forms a rounded bulb of swollen leaf stems topped with feathery, dill-like foliage. Direct sow seed a quarter inch deep in rich, moisture-retentive soil, and thin so bulbs have room to swell. Keep water steady and consider mounding soil around the base to blanch and sweeten the bulbs. Harvest before summer heat triggers bolting; both the bulb and the anise-scented fronds are useful in the kitchen.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring; tolerates summer heat for continuous harvest into fall.

directions: This classic heirloom chard produces broad, glossy green leaves with thick white stems and a mild, tender flavor for fresh or cooked dishes. Sow the seed clusters half an inch deep and thin seedlings to give each plant room to bush out. Harvest outer leaves regularly for cut-and-come-again production while the center keeps growing. Unusually heat and cold tolerant, it yields continuously from late spring well into fall with steady moisture.

planting.note: Sow after frost when soil is warm; cold, wet soil rots the seed.

directions: This All-America Selections bush lima is valued for resilience, drought tolerance, and heavy crops of large, buttery beans. Sow seed an inch to an inch and a half deep only once the soil has warmed, since limas rot in cold, wet ground. Grow in full sun with even moisture through flowering and pod fill. Pick pods when they feel plump and full for fresh shelling, or leave them to dry on the bush for storage beans.

planting.note: Direct sow every couple weeks in spring and fall for a steady supply.

directions: This elegant heirloom produces elongated roots with bright crimson tops and clean white tips, offering a tender, mildly peppery bite. Direct sow seed a half inch deep and keep the bed consistently moist for quick, uniform roots. Thin promptly so each radish has room, and succession sow every week or two for continuous harvest. Pull the roots young while crisp, as radishes left too long turn pithy and sharp; they excel in cool spring and fall weather.

planting.note: Sow after frost when soil warms; succession sow for extended harvest.

directions: This compact haricot vert produces slender, stringless pods with fine flavor on tidy dwarf bush plants ideal for small spaces and containers. Sow seed about an inch deep directly in warm soil, as beans dislike transplanting. Keep moisture even through flowering and pod set, and avoid handling wet foliage to reduce disease. Harvest the pencil-thin pods often while young and tender to keep the plants producing over several weeks.

planting.note: Sow after frost in warm soil; long vines need plenty of room.

directions: This Southern heirloom from the 1830s produces oblong, green-striped fruits of 25 to 35 pounds with sweet, rosy-red flesh. Sow seed an inch deep in warm, fertile soil and give the sprawling vines abundant space. Water consistently while fruits swell, then ease off as they near ripeness to concentrate sweetness. Look for a curled, dry tendril near the stem and a creamy ground spot as ripeness cues before cutting the melon.

planting.note: Sow in spring or late summer; flavor improves after fall frost.

directions: This time-tested Southern heirloom yields large, tender, blue-green leaves with a rich, traditional flavor and strong vigor. Sow seed a quarter to half an inch deep and thin plants so the broad leaves have room to develop. Harvest lower leaves as needed for cut-and-come-again picking, leaving the growing tip intact. Highly cold hardy, the greens sweeten noticeably after fall frost and produce reliably in both spring and fall gardens.

planting.note: Start indoors early and transplant into deep holes to blanch the stalks.

directions: This AAS-winning heirloom leek produces thick, mild, onion-flavored stalks that store well through cold weather. Start seed indoors about ten weeks before the last frost, then transplant the pencil-thick seedlings into deep dibbled holes to blanch the lower stems. Water steadily and hill soil around the shafts as they grow to lengthen the tender white portion. Harvest as needed through fall and winter, since the hardy plants tolerate hard freezes.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer for a fall crop.

directions: This classic heirloom spinach forms large, deep-green leaves with a mild, tender flavor, spreading widely as it grows. Direct sow seed half an inch deep in cool, fertile soil as early as it can be worked, since spinach bolts quickly in heat. Keep moisture even and thin plants so leaves size up fully. Harvest outer leaves or cut whole plants young, and sow again in late summer for a productive, cold-tolerant fall crop.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring or late summer; heat prompts bolting.

directions: This heirloom mustard combines ornamental burgundy-red leaves brushed with green and a bold, spicy flavor that mellows with cooking. Direct sow seed a quarter to half an inch deep and thin for baby leaves or fuller plants. Harvest young leaves for a milder bite in salads or let them mature for cooking greens. Grow in cool spring and fall weather with steady moisture, since heat pushes plants to bolt and turns the flavor sharp.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant for spring, or sow midsummer for fall heads.

directions: This early heirloom produces compact, round green heads quickly, making it a good fit for small gardens and short seasons. Start seed indoors five to six weeks before transplanting into firm, fertile soil. Water evenly and consistently, as swings in moisture can crack the maturing heads. Harvest as soon as heads feel solid, since the early type is prone to splitting if left too long; it succeeds in both spring and fall.

planting.note: Sow after frost in warm soil; plant in blocks for good pollination.

directions: This historic open-pollinated heirloom brought sweet yellow corn to the home table and still delivers rich, old-fashioned flavor. Sow seed an inch to an inch and a half deep in warm soil, planting in blocks of several short rows so wind pollination fills the ears. Provide plenty of water and nitrogen through tasseling and ear fill. Harvest when the silks brown and kernels are plump and milky, and cook or preserve promptly for the best flavor.

planting.note: Sow after frost when soil is warm; succession sow for extended yield.

directions: This classic yellow summer squash offers buttery flavor and tender texture on compact, bush-style plants that suit home gardens. Sow seed an inch deep in warm, fertile soil, spacing plants for good air movement. Keep moisture steady and harvest fruits young, while the curved necks are still small and the skin is tender. Frequent picking keeps the plants producing heavily, since mature fruits left on the plant slow new set.

planting.note: Sow after frost when soil warms; succession sow every few weeks.

directions: This productive yellow wax bean produces bright, stringless pods with tender texture on compact 15 to 20 inch bush plants. Sow seed about an inch deep directly in warm soil, since beans resent root disturbance. Keep moisture even during flowering and pod set, and avoid working among wet plants to limit disease. Pick pods often while young and slender for fresh eating or preserving, which keeps the plants setting new pods.

planting.note: Sow after frost when soil is warm; a single plant is very productive.

directions: This heirloom, open-pollinated variety produces bright yellow, cylindrical fruits with classic zucchini flavor on compact bush plants. Sow seed an inch deep in warm, fertile soil and give each plant room for air circulation. Water consistently at the base and harvest fruits young, around six to eight inches, for the best texture. Pick every day or two during peak season, since fruits enlarge fast and steady harvest keeps production high.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost once nights stay warm.

directions: This rare heirloom beefsteak produces creamy pale fruits of one to two pounds with sweet, low-acid, almost tropical flavor. Start seed indoors about six weeks before transplanting, then set hardened plants deep into rich, warm soil. As an indeterminate vine, it benefits from sturdy staking or caging and consistent watering to prevent cracking. Harvest when the pale fruits take on a faint blush and yield slightly to gentle pressure.

planting.note: Start indoors early; chilling young plants encourages first-year budding.

directions: This heirloom perennial grows into a bold 4 to 6 foot plant producing large green buds tipped with light purple. Start seed indoors early and give young plants a cool period, which helps them form buds in the first season. Transplant into rich, well-drained soil with steady moisture and ample space for the architectural foliage. Harvest buds while tight and firm before the scales open; in mild zones the plants return and yield for several years.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant, or grow as a fall crop in cool weather.

directions: This rare Italian heirloom forms vivid green curds with a mild, buttery flavor and reliable performance across seasons. Start seed indoors about six weeks ahead, then transplant into rich, evenly moist soil for uninterrupted growth. Consistent feeding and watering prevent stress that leads to loose or off-flavored heads. Cut heads while the green curds are still tight and firm for the best eating quality.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring and fall; succession sow for a continuous supply.

directions: This AAS award-winning oakleaf-type lettuce produces frilly green leaves that stay sweet and tender even in warm weather. Direct sow seed shallowly and press lightly into moist soil, since lettuce needs light to germinate. Thin plants for full heads or harvest as loose-leaf, cut-and-come-again greens. More heat tolerant and slow to bolt than many lettuces, it extends the salad season with steady moisture and light afternoon shade in summer.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant for spring, or sow midsummer for fall.

directions: This traditional Italian heirloom is the familiar garden broccoli, producing large blue-green central heads followed by abundant side shoots. Start seed indoors about six weeks before transplanting into rich, firm soil. Keep water and nutrients steady, since stress can cause premature buttoning. Cut the main head while the buds are still tight, then keep harvesting the smaller side shoots for weeks of continued production.

planting.note: Sow after frost when soil is warm; tolerates heat well.

directions: This heat-tolerant heirloom produces smooth, straight fruits with mottled silver-grey skin on compact, heavy-yielding bush plants. Sow seed an inch deep in warm, fertile soil and space plants for good airflow. Water at the base to keep foliage dry and harvest fruits young, around six to eight inches, for tender flesh. Pick frequently during the peak of summer, as fruits size up quickly and steady harvest sustains production.
